We don't just show you code—we explain the reasoning behind architectural decisions so you can apply these patterns to your own unique challenges.

Each lesson includes hands-on practice with real-world examples. You'll see patterns demonstrated in production-quality code, not just toy examples. This practical approach ensures you can immediately apply what you learn to your own projects.

## What You'll Walk Away With

By completing this course, you'll have a comprehensive understanding of React design patterns that you can apply throughout your career. You'll know how to structure components for maximum reusability, when to reach for different state management patterns, how to optimize performance with advanced hook techniques, and how to create polished user experiences with proven UI patterns.

More importantly, you'll develop the judgment to know which pattern to use in different situations. This isn't about memorizing solutions—it's about understanding principles that help you make better architectural decisions every day.

The patterns you learn here are used in production applications at companies building real products. They're battle-tested solutions that have proven themselves across countless React codebases. Whether you're building your own projects, contributing to a team, or preparing for technical interviews, these patterns will make you a more effective React developer.

modules.name: Foundational Patterns
modules.description: Learn the fundamentals of design patterns in React by practicing custom hooks, state management, and component organization

lessons.name: What are Design Patterns?
lessons.description: Learn what design patterns are and why they are essential for building maintainable React applications
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll discover what design patterns are and understand their crucial role in creating maintainable, scalable React applications. Design patterns are proven solutions to common problems that developers face when building software, and they help us write more organized and reusable code.

Your coding tasks will be to explore the code editor to get familiar with the React environment, as there are no specific code requirements for this introductory lesson.

objectives.name: Understand what design patterns are and why they matter in software development (e.g., code reusability, maintainability, team collaboration)
Give a brief history of design patterns

Learn how design patterns apply specifically to React applications (react can be written in many different ways, and design patterns help us write code that is more organized and reusable. Some ways are more efficient than others) (mention hard to understand code, big components, too many hooks and JSX, and all the other bad things one encounters in the wild)

Let the user know that in this course we're going to be looking at a wide variety of design patterns. And the goal is really to have those in our knowledge belt to be able to pick and choose the ones that make the most sense for the problem that we're facing. And that we are in fact going to be building a skill and we're going to be still building React applications, which are going to do the same thing. But now the way we're going to do it with our code is going to be much better, much more robust, much more scalable. And it's going to make us better developers and our team more productive.

tasks.name: Submit the code editor – no code required

lessons.name: Custom Hooks
lessons.description: Learn how to create custom hooks to extract and reuse stateful logic across components
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to effectively use custom hooks, which are a powerful design pattern in React that allows you to extract component logic into reusable functions. Custom hooks let you share stateful logic between components without changing their structure, making your code more modular and easier to maintain.

Your coding tasks will be creating a simple custom hook that manages a counter state, demonstrating how to extract logic from components into reusable functions.

objectives.name: Understand what custom hooks are and how they must follow React's rules of hooks (go over rules and mention that they are just functions that return a value and that they are a way to reuse stateful logic)
Learn how custom hooks enable code reuse and separation of concerns (and why you need it to avoid code duplication and other issues)
Recognize when to create a custom hook versus keeping logic in a component (mention that it's a trade-off and you need to make the decision based on the problem at hand)

tasks.name: Create a state variable called `count` and initialize it to `0` using the `useState` hook
Create a function called `increment` that increments the `count` state by `1` using the state setter function
Return an object with `count` and `increment` properties
Use the `useCounter` hook and access the `count` and `increment` function in a destructured object
Replace the `count` and `increment` placeholders with your `useCounter` hook implementation

lessons.name: Lifting State Up
lessons.description: Learn how to share state between components by moving it to their common parent
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about lifting state up, a fundamental pattern in React for sharing state between sibling components. When multiple components need access to the same state, you move that state to their closest common ancestor and pass it down as props.

Your coding tasks will be moving state from a child component to a parent component and passing the state down as props to enable communication between sibling components.

objectives.name: Understand when and why to lift state up to a parent component (give examples)
Learn how to pass state and state setters as props to child components
Recognize the trade-offs between local state and lifted state (give examples of both and even ask the user to think about the trade-offs)

tasks.name: Remove the `name` and `setName` state from the `NameInput` component
Implement the `name` and `setName` state variables inside the `App` component
Have the `NameDisplay` component receive `name` as props
Have the `NameInput` component receive `name` and `setName` as props
Pass `name` as props to the `NameDisplay` component
Render the `name` from props in the greeting
Pass the `name` and `setName` as props to the `NameInput` component

lessons.name: Colocating State
lessons.description: Learn how to organize state by keeping it close to where it is used for better maintainability
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about colocating state, an organizational pattern that helps keep your components maintainable by placing state as close as possible to where it's used. This pattern reduces complexity and makes components easier to understand and modify.

Your coding tasks will be moving state from a parent component down to a child component where it's actually needed, demonstrating how colocation improves code organization.

objectives.name: Understand the principle of state colocation and why it improves maintainability
Learn when to move state closer to where it's used versus keeping it in parent components (refer back to the previous lesson about lifting state up)
Recognize how colocation reduces prop drilling and component complexity, and allows reuse of components

tasks.name: Have the `ToggleSection` receive no props
Implement the `isVisible` and `setIsVisible` state variables inside the `ToggleSection` component
Remove the `isVisible` and `setIsVisible` state from the `App` component
Remove the `isVisible` and `setIsVisible` props from the `ToggleSection` component

lessons.name: State Machine Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to manage complex state with a simple state machine using a single status variable
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the state machine pattern, which replaces multiple boolean state variables with a single status variable that can have different values. This simplifies state management and prevents impossible states like being both loading and having an error at the same time.

Your coding tasks will be refactoring multiple boolean states into a single status state and updating the component logic to use this cleaner approach.

objectives.name: Understand what a state machine is and why it simplifies state management (prevents impossible states, clearer logic flow)
Learn how to replace multiple boolean states with a single status variable (loading, error, ready states become one status)
Recognize when to use state machines versus separate boolean states (when states are mutually exclusive or follow a clear flow)

tasks.name: Replace the separate boolean states with a single `status` state with a value of `"ready"`
Replace the if check by checking that status is `"ready"` and set the status to `"loading"`
Replace the else if check by checking that status is `"loading"` and set the status to `"error"`
Replace the else if check by checking that status is `"error"` and set the status to `"ready"`
Update the render logic to check the `status` value instead of separate boolean states

modules.name: Component Patterns
modules.description: Learn how to build scalable component architectures with composition patterns and design principles

lessons.name: Single Responsibility Principle
lessons.description: Learn how to keep components focused by ensuring each component has only one reason to change
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) and how it applies to React components. This principle states that each component should have only one reason to change, meaning it should focus on doing one thing well. Following SRP makes components easier to understand, test, and maintain.

Your coding tasks will be refactoring a large component that violates SRP by breaking it down into smaller, focused components and extracting logic into a custom hook.

objectives.name: Understand what the Single Responsibility Principle is and what it means for React components (each component should have one clear purpose and one reason to change)
Learn how to identify when a component is doing too much (e.g., handling multiple concerns like data fetching, UI rendering, and business logic)
Recognize the benefits of small, focused components (easier testing, better reusability, improved maintainability, and clearer code organization)

tasks.name: Extract the user data fetching logic into a custom hook called `useUser`
Create a `UserProfile` component that only handles displaying user information
Create a `UserActions` component that only handles user action buttons
Refactor the `UserDashboard` component to use the new hook and components

lessons.name: Presentational vs Container Components
lessons.description: Learn how to separate presentation logic from business logic using presentational and container components
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the distinction between presentational and container components, a pattern that helps separate concerns in React applications. Presentational components focus purely on how things look, while container components handle how things work by managing state and business logic.

Your coding tasks will be extracting business logic from a mixed component into a custom hook and creating a clean presentational component that receives data through props.

objectives.name: Understand the difference between presentational and container components (presentational components focus on UI rendering while container components handle state and logic)
Learn when to use each pattern and how they work together (presentational components are reusable and testable, container components provide data and behavior)
Recognize how this separation improves component reusability and testability (presentational components can be used in different contexts with different data sources)

tasks.name: Extract the todo management logic into a custom hook called `useTodos`
Create a presentational `TodoList` component that receives `todos` and `onToggle` as props
Refactor the `TodoContainer` to use the hook and render the presentational component

lessons.name: Smart vs Dumb Components
lessons.description: Learn the difference between UI components and feature components in application architecture
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the distinction between smart and dumb components, which builds on the presentational vs container pattern from the previous lesson. UI components (dumb) are purely for displaying interface elements, while feature components (smart) handle interactions and business logic. This creates a clear hierarchy where pages contain feature components, which in turn use UI components.

Your coding tasks will be creating simple UI components for displaying data and a feature component that orchestrates them with interactive behavior.

objectives.name: Understand the difference between UI components and feature components (UI components display data without logic, feature components handle interactions and coordinate UI components)
Learn how these components fit into application architecture (pages contain feature components, feature components contain UI components, creating a clear hierarchy)
Recognize how this pattern scales to build complex applications (feature components can be reused across pages, UI components can be reused across features, creating modular and maintainable code)

tasks.name: Create a dumb `Card` UI component that receives `title` and `content` as props
Create a dumb `Button` UI component that receives `children` and `onClick` as props
Implement the `article` state and `setArticle` setter function inside the `ArticleFeature` component
Implement the `handleLike` function inside the `ArticleFeature` component
Render the `Card` component in the `ArticleFeature` component
Render the `Button` component in the `ArticleFeature` component
Render the `ArticleFeature` component in the main `App` component

lessons.name: Children Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to use the children prop to create flexible and reusable layout components
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the children pattern, which allows you to pass components as children through the `children` prop instead of rendering them directly. This pattern prevents unnecessary re-renders because the parent component doesn't own the child components' state, and it provides flexibility by allowing any component to be passed as a slot.

Your coding tasks will be creating layout components that use the children prop to encapsulate structure and behavior while remaining agnostic about their content.

objectives.name: Understand how the children prop works and why it prevents unnecessary re-renders (children are created by the parent's parent, so state changes in the wrapper don't recreate them, and how it's a common fix for expensive re-renders)
Learn how children provide flexibility as slots for any component (the wrapper component doesn't need to know what specific components it will render)
Recognize when to use children vs props for component composition (children for layout and behavior encapsulation, props for specific data passing)

tasks.name: Create a `Wrapper` component that receives `children` and `showContent` as props
Render the `Wrapper` component and pass `showContent` as a normal prop and `content` as children

lessons.name: Function Children Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to pass functions as children to share data between parent and child components
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the function children pattern, where instead of passing JSX as children, you pass a function that receives arguments from the parent component. This pattern allows the parent to share data with its children while keeping the rendering logic flexible.

Your coding tasks will be creating a component that calls its children as a function and passes data to it, demonstrating how this pattern enables data sharing with flexible rendering.

objectives.name: Understand how function children work (children is a function that receives arguments from the parent)
Learn how to call children as a function and pass data to it (parent calls children with arguments)
Recognize when to use function children versus regular children (when you need to share data from parent to child)
Compare this pattern to hooks and how sometimes hooks are better and why (more modern, composable, flexible)

tasks.name: Create a `DataProvider` component that receives `children` as a function and calls it with `user` data
Use the `DataProvider` component with a function as children that receives `user` and renders it

lessons.name: Render Props Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to pass rendering logic as props to create flexible and reusable components
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the render props pattern, which allows you to pass rendering logic as a prop to a component. This pattern is similar to function children but uses a named prop instead of the children prop, making it explicit what kind of rendering function is expected.

Your coding tasks will be creating a component that takes a render function as a prop and uses it to render each item in a list with different presentations.

objectives.name: Understand what render props are and how they differ from function children (named props vs children prop for passing render functions)
Talk about the key and how it must be provided either inside the component that uses renderItem, or in the renderItem function
Learn how render props enable flexible component reuse (same data logic with different presentations)
Recognize when to use render props versus other composition patterns (when you need explicit, named rendering logic)

tasks.name: Create a `List` component that takes `items` and `renderItem` as props and renders a list of items by calling the `renderItem` function for each item
Render the `List` component with the items and a `renderItem` function that returns a `li` tag with the item

lessons.name: Compound Components Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to create compound components that work together to provide flexible and reusable interfaces
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the compound components pattern, which allows you to create a set of components that work together but can be composed in different ways. This pattern provides flexibility by letting users arrange sub-components in different orders while maintaining the overall functionality.

Your coding tasks will be creating a simple compound component system and using it in different configurations to demonstrate its flexibility and reusability.

objectives.name: Understand what compound components are and how they work together (multiple components that share behavior but can be arranged flexibly)
Learn how compound components provide flexibility in component composition (users can arrange sub-components in different orders)
Understand how to create a context for a compound component (show example of simple context, passing data to children, and accessing it with custom hooks)
Recognize when to use compound components versus other patterns (when you need flexible composition with shared behavior)

Mention that in React 19, we don't need to use `.Provider` extension and can use the context directly. We also don't need to use the `useContext` hook and can use the `use` hook instead. ALLOW THE USER TO USE WHICHEVER THEY WANT.

tasks.name: Create a `PostCard` component that receives `children` and a `post` as props and provides the `post` as context to its children
Create `PostCard.Header` sub-component that renders the `post.title` in an `h2` tag using the `usePost` hook
Create `PostCard.Body` sub-component that renders the `post.content` in a `p` tag using the `usePost` hook
Create `PostCard.Footer` sub-component that renders the `post.author` in a `p` tag using the `usePost` hook
Render the `PostCard` component with the `post` as props, and the `PostCard.Header`, `PostCard.Body`, and `PostCard.Footer` sub-components
Render the `PostCard` component with the `post` as props, but only render the `PostCard.Body` sub-component

lessons.name: Higher-Order Components
lessons.description: Learn about Higher-Order Components (HOCs) for component logic reuse in legacy React code
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about Higher-Order Components (HOCs), which are functions that take a component and return a new component with additional functionality. While HOCs are mostly replaced by hooks in modern React, you'll still encounter them in legacy codebases and some libraries.

Your coding tasks will be creating a simple `withLoading` HOC that adds loading functionality to any component and using it to enhance a basic component.

objectives.name: Understand what Higher-Order Components are and how they work (functions that enhance components with additional props or behavior)
Learn why HOCs were important before hooks and why they're less common now (hooks provide a simpler way to share logic between components, show comparison examples for clarity)
Recognize HOCs in legacy code and third-party libraries (you'll encounter them even if you don't write new ones)

tasks.name: Create a `withUser` HOC that takes a component and returns it with `user` as a prop
Create an `EnhancedUserProfile` component that uses the `withUser` HOC and passes `UserProfile` as the component
Render the `EnhancedUserProfile` component

lessons.name: Dynamic Component Loading
lessons.description: Learn how to lazy load components dynamically based on user interaction to improve performance
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about dynamic component loading using React's `lazy` function and `Suspense`. This pattern allows you to load components only when they're needed, reducing the initial bundle size and improving app startup performance.

Your coding tasks will be converting a regular component import to a lazy import and wrapping it with `Suspense` to handle the loading state.

objectives.name: Understand what dynamic component loading is and why it improves performance (smaller initial bundle, faster startup, load components on demand)
Learn how `React.lazy` and `Suspense` work together (lazy creates a lazy component, Suspense handles loading states) (learn the API of Suspense and fallback props)
Recognize realistic use cases like modals, admin panels, or feature-specific components that aren't always needed

tasks.name: Convert the regular `Dashboard` import to use the `lazy` function
Wrap the `Dashboard` component with `Suspense` and provide a `p` tag with the message `Loading...` as the fallback

lessons.name: Virtualization Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to use virtualization to efficiently render large lists with react-window
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll discover the virtualization pattern using the `react-window` library, which efficiently renders large lists by only showing visible items in the viewport. This dramatically improves performance when dealing with thousands of list items by avoiding the creation of unnecessary DOM nodes.

Your coding tasks will be replacing a regular list with a virtualized `FixedSizeList` component and creating a simple row component to render each item.

objectives.name: Understand what virtualization is and why it improves performance for large lists (only renders visible items, reduces DOM nodes)
Learn how `react-window` works with `FixedSizeList` for lists with uniform item heights
Recognize when to use virtualization (lists with hundreds or thousands of items)

tasks.name: Create a `Row` component that receives `index`, `items`, and `style` as props and renders the item
Replace the regular list rendering with `List` component

modules.name: Hook Patterns
modules.description: Learn advanced hook patterns for efficient state management and performance optimization

lessons.name: Deriving State
lessons.description: Learn how to derive state from existing state instead of creating unnecessary state variables
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about deriving state, which means calculating values from existing state instead of storing them in separate state variables. This pattern eliminates unnecessary `useEffect` hooks and reduces the risk of state getting out of sync.

Your coding tasks will be removing an unnecessary state variable and `useEffect` hook by deriving the full name directly from the first and last name states.

objectives.name: Understand when state should be derived instead of stored (when one piece of state can be calculated from another)
Learn how deriving state eliminates unnecessary `useEffect` hooks and prevents synchronization bugs
Recognize the performance benefits of deriving state (no extra renders, simpler component logic)

tasks.name: Remove the `fullName` state variable
Remove the `useEffect` that updates `fullName`
Create a new variable called `fullName` that is derived from `firstName` and `lastName`

lessons.name: Debounce
lessons.description: Learn how to use the useDebounce hook to delay expensive operations until user input stabilizes
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about debouncing, which delays executing a function until after a specified time has passed since it was last called. This is useful for expensive operations like API calls or search queries that you don't want to run on every keystroke.

Your coding tasks will be using a `useDebounce` hook to delay logging user input until they stop typing for a specified amount of time.

objectives.name: Understand what debouncing is and why it's useful (preventing excessive function calls during rapid user input)

Learn how `useDebounce` hooks work (they return a delayed version of a value that only updates after a specified delay, worth it to show example of the hook and its usage, so user knows how to use it and pass params)
Recognize common use cases like search inputs, API calls, and resize handlers

tasks.name: Create a `debouncedValue` state variable using the `useState` hook with `value` as the initial value
Create a `useEffect` hook that sets the `debouncedValue` state variable to the `value` after the `delay` using `setTimeout`
Return the `debouncedValue` state variable
Use the `useDebounce` hook to create a `debouncedSearchTerm` variable using the `searchTerm` state and a `500ms` delay
Render the `debouncedSearchTerm` inside the curly braces

lessons.name: Prioritize rendering with useDeferredValue
lessons.description: Learn how to use useDeferredValue to defer expensive updates and improve user experience
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about `useDeferredValue`, which allows you to defer updating a value until more urgent updates have finished. This helps keep the UI responsive by allowing high-priority updates (like user input) to render before lower-priority updates (like expensive calculations).

Your coding tasks will be using `useDeferredValue` to defer updating an expensive list rendering while keeping the input field responsive.

objectives.name: Understand what `useDeferredValue` does and why it improves performance (defers less important updates to keep UI responsive)
Learn how it differs from debouncing (useDeferredValue is about render priority, debouncing is about timing)
Recognize when to use it (expensive rendering that shouldn't block user interactions)

tasks.name: Use `useDeferredValue` to create a `deferredQuery` variable using the `query` state
Replace the `SlowList` `query` prop with `deferredQuery`

lessons.name: Interrupt rendering with useTransition
lessons.description: Learn how to use useTransition to mark updates as non-urgent and show loading states
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about `useTransition`, which allows you to mark state updates as non-urgent transitions. This keeps the UI responsive during expensive updates by allowing React to interrupt them for more urgent updates, and provides a loading state you can use to show feedback to users.

Your coding tasks will be using `useTransition` to wrap an async state update and displaying a loading indicator while the transition is pending.

objectives.name: Understand what `useTransition` does and how it improves user experience (marks updates as non-urgent, provides loading states)
Learn how it works with the `startTransition` function and `isPending` state
Learn how to nest startTransition inside another startTransition when using async state updates (mention that this will be fixed in later React versions)
Recognize when to use it (expensive state updates that shouldn't block user interactions)

tasks.name: Use the `useTransition` hook to get `isPending` and `startTransition` variables
Wrap the `asyncFunction` function call and state update with `startTransition`
Render a `p` tag with the message `"Loading..."` when `isPending` is `true`

lessons.name: Effect Separation Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to separate multiple concerns in useEffect by using multiple effects instead of one
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the effect separation pattern, which means using multiple `useEffect` hooks instead of combining different concerns into a single effect. This makes your code more maintainable and allows each effect to have its own dependency array.

Your coding tasks will be splitting a single `useEffect` that handles both document title updates and logging into two separate effects with their own dependencies.

objectives.name: Understand why multiple effects are better than one large effect (separation of concerns, clearer dependencies, easier maintenance, porting to custom hooks)
Learn how to identify when an effect is doing too much (multiple unrelated operations with different dependencies, refer back to SRP)
Recognize that each effect can have its own dependency array and cleanup logic

tasks.name: Create a separate `useEffect` for updating the document title that depends only on `name`
Create a separate `useEffect` for logging that depends only on `count`
Remove the original combined `useEffect`

lessons.name: First Render Detection
lessons.description: Learn how to detect the first render using a ref instead of useEffect for better performance
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about first render detection using a ref pattern instead of `useEffect`. You create a ref that starts as `false`, check it during render, and if it's false you run your first-render logic and set it to `true`. This is more efficient than using `useEffect` with an empty dependency array.

Your coding tasks will be implementing this pattern to show a welcome message only on the first render without using `useEffect`.

objectives.name: Understand why using a ref is better than `useEffect` for first render detection (runs synchronously during render, no extra render cycle)
Learn how the ref pattern works (starts false, check during render, set to true after first use)
Recognize when this pattern is useful (initialization logic that should run once during the first render)

tasks.name: Create a ref called `isFirstRender` initialized to `true` using the `useRef` hook
During rendering, add an if statement that checks if `isFirstRender.current` is `true`
Inside the if statement, log a `"First render"` message and set `isFirstRender.current` to `false`

lessons.name: Custom Hook Composition
lessons.description: Learn how to combine multiple custom hooks into one powerful hook for complex functionality
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about custom hook composition, which means combining multiple custom hooks together to create more powerful and reusable functionality. This pattern helps organize complex logic by breaking it into smaller, focused hooks that can be combined as needed.

Your coding tasks will be creating a search functionality by composing separate hooks for input management and filtering logic into one comprehensive search hook.

objectives.name: Understand how to break complex logic into multiple focused custom hooks (separation of concerns at the hook level – go beyond learning what a custom hook is, focus on the design pattern at scale)
Learn how to combine multiple hooks into one comprehensive hook (composition over complexity)

Recognize when to use hook composition versus single large hooks (when logic has distinct but related concerns, mention how you can have a big hook for a feature that has many smaller hooks inside it as an example)

tasks.name: Create a `useInput` hook that manages a state variable `query` and a `setQuery` handler
Create a `useFilter` hook that takes `items` and `query` as arguments, and returns a memoized array of filtered items using `useMemo`
Create a `useSearch` hook that uses the `useInput` and `useFilter` hooks
Replace the `useState` hook and the `filteredItems` variable with the returned values from the `useSearch` hook

lessons.name: Context Selector
lessons.description: Learn how to use context selectors to prevent unnecessary re-renders when context values change
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about context selectors using the `use-context-selector` library, which allows components to subscribe to only specific parts of a context value. This prevents unnecessary re-renders when other parts of the context change that the component doesn't care about.

Your coding tasks will be replacing regular `useContext` with `useContextSelector` to optimize performance by selecting only the specific data each component needs.

objectives.name: Understand the performance problem with regular context (all consumers re-render when any part of context changes)
Learn how context selectors solve this by allowing components to subscribe to specific values only
Recognize when to use context selectors (when context has multiple values and components only need specific ones)

Mention how specifically the use-context-selector library works and that the code is still going to run but it won't get committed. So if we put a useEffect to console.log when the component is rendering, it's only going to show when the value changes. But if we just console.log it directly in the component without the useEffect, it's going to run even if the value doesn't change

tasks.name: Replace the `useContext` hook in `NameComponent` with `useContextSelector` and select only the `name` using a selector function
Replace the `useContext` hook in `CountComponent` with `useContextSelector` and select only the `count` using a selector function

lessons.name: UseImperativeHandle
lessons.description: Learn how to expose imperative methods from child components to parent components using refs
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about `useImperativeHandle`, which allows child components to expose specific methods to their parent components through a ref. This pattern is useful when you need to call functions on a child component imperatively, like focusing an input.

Your coding tasks will be creating a custom input component that exposes a focus method to its parent using `useImperativeHandle`.

objectives.name: Understand what `useImperativeHandle` does and when to use it (exposing specific methods from child to parent)

Mention how pre React 19, `forwardRef` was used pass the ref, but now it's not needed anymore we can pass the ref directly to the component as a normal prop (not as an extra or separate prop). Just through `ref` like any other prop. IMPORTANT: REFS CAN BE DESTRUCTURED LIKE NORMAL PROPS IN REACT 19+. FORWARD REF HAS BEEN REMOVED. DO NOT TELL THE USER TO MAKE THE REF A SEPARATE PROP. THEY ARE NOW COMPLELY NORMAL PROPS.

Recognize common use cases like focusing inputs, scrolling elements, or triggering animations (mention that we can even do it through a context to provide broader access to the inner workings of a component)
Mention that this is the opposite of React's top-down data flow. But sometimes, there are valid reasons to do it (show examples)

tasks.name: Create a `CustomInput` component that receives a `ref` and `...rest` props
Use `useImperativeHandle` to expose a `focusInner` method that focuses the input using `localRef.current.focus()`
Return an input element with the `localRef` as the ref and spread the `...rest` props
Create a `handleFocus` function that focuses the input by calling the `focusInner` method on the `inputRef`

lessons.name: Latest Ref Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to use refs to access the latest values in useEffect without causing re-renders
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the latest ref pattern, which solves the problem of accessing the latest values in `useEffect` without adding them to the dependency array. You create a ref to store the function and another `useEffect` with no dependencies that runs on every render to keep the ref updated.

Your coding tasks will be implementing this pattern with an event listener that needs access to the latest count value without causing the effect to re-run on every count change.

objectives.name: Understand the problem of dependency arrays in `useEffect` (stale closures, functions capture values at the time they're created, unnecessary re-renders, potential infinite loops)
Learn how the latest ref pattern solves this using refs and a synchronizing effect
Recognize how refs provide imperative access to always get the latest values (even in an event listener set only once)

tasks.name: Create a ref using `useRef` called `onClickRef` to store the latest `onClick` value
Create a `useEffect` with no dependencies that updates `onClickRef.current` on every render with the latest `onClick` value
Use `onClickRef.current` in the click handler instead of the stale `onClick` value
Remove `onClick` from the dependency array of the `useEffect`

lessons.name: Abandon Render Pattern
lessons.description: Learn the abandon render pattern as an alternative to usePrevious hooks for tracking previous values
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the abandon render pattern, which is the official React-recommended way to track previous values instead of using `usePrevious` hooks. You use an if statement during render to check if a value changed, and if so, you update state mid-render, causing React to abandon the current render and start fresh with the new values.

Your coding tasks will be implementing this pattern to track the previous count value and display both current and previous values without using a `usePrevious` hook.

objectives.name: Understand why `usePrevious` hooks are not recommended (they can cause timing issues and are not officially supported)
Learn how the abandon render pattern works (if statement during render that sets state when values differ)
Recognize that this pattern is documented in the official React documentation as the preferred approach (even though it's weird)

tasks.name: Create a state variable called `prevCount` to store the previous `count` value
During rendering, add an `if` statement that checks if `count` is different from `prevCount`
Set `prevCount` to the current `count` value inside the `if` statement to abandon the render

lessons.name: Use Hook with Context
lessons.description: Learn how to use the modern use hook for reading context conditionally in React 19
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll discover the modern `use` hook introduced in React 19, which provides a more flexible way to read context values compared to `useContext`. The `use` hook can be called conditionally inside if statements and loops, making it more versatile for dynamic component behavior.

Your coding tasks will be replacing `useContext` with the new `use` hook and implementing conditional context reading to demonstrate its flexibility.

objectives.name: Understand the benefits of the `use` hook over `useContext` (can be called conditionally)
Learn how to use the `use` hook to read context values
Explore conditional context reading with the `use` hook inside if statements

tasks.name: Replace `useContext(ThemeContext)` with `use(ThemeContext)` in the `Button` component
Move the `use(ThemeContext)` call inside the `if (show)` condition in the `Button` component
Remove the `.Provider` extension and use `ThemeContext` directly

lessons.name: Use Hook with Promises
lessons.description: Learn how to use the use hook with promises and Suspense for handling asynchronous data
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn how the `use` hook works with promises to handle asynchronous data in React 19. When you pass a promise to `use`, it integrates with Suspense boundaries to show loading states while the promise is pending, and displays the resolved data once available.

Your coding tasks will be creating a simple promise and using the `use` hook to read its value within a Suspense boundary.

objectives.name: Understand how the `use` hook works with promises for asynchronous data
Learn how `use` integrates with Suspense to handle loading states
Recognize the pattern of passing promises to components and reading them with `use`

Mention that this is usually used for streaming data from server components to client components, or even on client side (show examples of server components creating promises and passing them to client components, and benefits of this (not having to wait for the entire page to load))

tasks.name: Use the `use` hook to read the `messagePromise` in the `Message` component
Display the resolved message content in the paragraph element
Pass the `messagePromise` to the `Message` component as props

lessons.name: useEffectEvent Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to use useEffectEvent to read latest values in Effects without causing re-synchronization
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about useEffectEvent, a new React hook that solves a common problem: reading the latest props or state in an Effect without making those values reactive. Sometimes you want an Effect to re-run only when certain values change, but still access the latest values of other props or state without causing the Effect to re-run. useEffectEvent lets you extract non-reactive logic from your Effects.

Your coding task will be refactoring an Effect that currently re-runs unnecessarily whenever itemCount changes, even though we only want it to run when the URL changes.

objectives.name: Understand the difference between reactive and non-reactive logic in Effects (talk about functions, objects, arrays, etc. and how they are reactive and must go in the dependency array if used in the Effect)
Learn when to use useEffectEvent to prevent unnecessary Effect re-runs (when you want to read the latest values of other props or state without causing the Effect to re-run)

tasks.name: Create an effect event called `onVisit` using the `useEffectEvent` hook
Update the `useEffect` to replace `trackPageView` with `onVisit` and pass the url as argument
Remove `itemCount` from the dependency array

modules.name: UI Patterns
modules.description: Learn how to build good user experiences by mastering loading states, errors, and proven UI patterns

lessons.name: Error Boundary Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to handle JavaScript errors in React components using error boundaries
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about error boundaries, which are React components that catch JavaScript errors anywhere in their child component tree and display a fallback UI instead of crashing the entire app. Error boundaries help create more resilient applications.

Your coding tasks will be wrapping a component that throws an error with an error boundary to prevent the app from crashing.

objectives.name: Understand what error boundaries are and why they're important (prevent entire app crashes from component errors)
Learn how to use the `react-error-boundary` library for simple error handling (mention that it's the only use case left for class components in modern react)
Recognize when and where to place error boundaries in your component tree (around risky components or feature boundaries)
Mention that a lot of frameworks or navigation libraries have their own error boundaries automatically applied even if you don't see it. So it's everywhere and standard practice

tasks.name: Create a `FallbackComponent` that receives `error` and `resetErrorBoundary` as props
Return the fallback UI using the `error` and `resetErrorBoundary` props
Add a button with the text `Reset` that calls `resetErrorBoundary` on click
Wrap the `BuggyComponent` with `ErrorBoundary` from react-error-boundary
Provide the `FallbackComponent` as the `fallback` prop
Provide a `onReset` prop that sets `shouldError` to `false`

lessons.name: Guard Clause Rendering
lessons.description: Learn how to use early returns to handle loading and error states cleanly
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about guard clause rendering, which uses early returns to exit the render function when data is missing, invalid, or still loading. This pattern keeps your main render logic clean and separates concerns by handling edge cases first.

Your coding tasks will be adding guard clauses that return early for loading and error states, and creating separate components for these states.

objectives.name: Understand what guard clauses are and how they simplify component logic (early returns that handle edge cases first, mention that this is a normal programing pattern)
Learn how to separate loading and error states into their own components (component separation for different UI states)
Recognize when to use guard clauses versus conditional rendering (guard clauses for simple states, conditional rendering for complex logic)

tasks.name: Create a `LoadingComponent` that returns a `p` tag with `Loading...`
Create an `ErrorComponent` that returns a `p` tag with `Something went wrong!`
Add a guard clause that returns `LoadingComponent` if `loading` is true
Add a guard clause that returns `ErrorComponent` if `error` is true

lessons.name: Skeleton & Placeholder Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to show skeleton placeholders while content is loading to improve perceived performance
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about skeleton and placeholder patterns, which show the structure of content while it's loading. This improves perceived performance by giving users a visual indication that content is coming and showing them what to expect.

Your coding tasks will be creating a simple skeleton component and displaying it while content is loading instead of a generic loading message.

objectives.name: Understand what skeleton loading is and why it improves user experience (shows content structure, reduces perceived loading time)
Recognize when to use skeletons versus loading spinners (skeletons for content with known structure, spinners for unknown wait times)
Learn how to create simple skeleton components that match your content structure

tasks.name: Create a `ProfileSkeleton` component with placeholder divs for name and email
Add a ternary operator that shows `ProfileSkeleton` when `loading` is true, otherwise shows `Profile`

lessons.name: Empty State Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to create meaningful empty states when lists or content areas have no data
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about empty state patterns, which provide helpful messaging and guidance when lists or content areas are empty. Good empty states tell users why the area is empty and often suggest actions they can take.

Your coding tasks will be creating a dedicated empty state component and displaying it when a list has no items instead of showing nothing.

objectives.name: Understand why empty states are important for user experience (prevent confusion, provide guidance, suggest actions)
Learn how to create dedicated empty state components (separate components for different empty scenarios)
Recognize when to show empty states versus loading states (empty when no data exists, loading when data is being fetched)

tasks.name: Create an `EmptyTodos` component that returns a `p` tag with `"No todos yet. Click the button to add one."`
Add a ternary operator that shows the `EmptyTodos` component when the todos array is empty, otherwise shows the todos

lessons.name: Optimistic UI Updates
lessons.description: Learn how to use useOptimistic to update the UI immediately before server responses
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about optimistic UI updates using the `useOptimistic` hook, which allows you to update the UI immediately while an async operation is pending. This makes the app feel faster and more responsive by showing the expected result before the server confirms it.

Your coding tasks will be using `useOptimistic` to immediately show a new todo item while a mock async function simulates saving it to the server.

objectives.name: Understand what optimistic updates are and why they improve user experience (immediate feedback, faster perceived performance)
Recognize when to use optimistic updates (operations that are likely to succeed, like adding items or toggling states)
Learn how `useOptimistic` works (provides optimistic state that reverts if the operation fails)
Go over how useOptimistic works with useTransition (how it's designed to work with React's concurrent rendering)

tasks.name: Use `useOptimistic` to create optimistic todos state
Add the new todo optimistically when the form is submitted
Render the optimistic state using `optimisticTodos` instead of `todos`

lessons.name: Partial Rendering of Available Data
lessons.description: Learn how to render available data gracefully even when some parts fail to load
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about partial rendering, which allows you to show available data even when some parts fail to load. For example, you can show a user's name and email even if their avatar image fails to load, providing a better user experience than showing nothing at all.

Your coding tasks will be creating components that gracefully handle missing or failed data by showing available information and fallbacks for unavailable parts.

objectives.name: Understand the principle of graceful degradation (show what you can, fallback for what you can't)
Learn how to separate data concerns so failures in one part don't affect others (independent data loading and error handling)

Recall how we handled error boundaries in previous lessons as well as early return with guard clauses for loading and error states. This is similar, but we're instead going to render the available partial data instead of a full fallback UI
Recognize when to use partial rendering (when different data sources have different reliability or loading times)

tasks.name: Create an `Avatar` component that returns an `img` tag with the `src` set to the `avatarUrl`, or a fallback image if the `avatarUrl` is null
Render the `Avatar` component with the user's `avatarUrl`

lessons.name: Variant Pattern
lessons.description: Learn how to use variants to create flexible component styling with predefined options
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the variant pattern, which uses an object to map variant names to style properties. Instead of passing style objects directly, you pass a variant prop and the component looks up the appropriate styles from a predefined variants object.

Your coding tasks will be creating a button component with variant-based styling and using it with different variants to show how this pattern simplifies component APIs.

objectives.name: Understand how the variant pattern simplifies component styling APIs (one prop instead of multiple style props)
Learn how to create a variants object that maps names to style properties
Recognize when to use variants versus direct style props (variants for predefined options, style props for custom styling)

tasks.name: Create a `variants` object that return an object with the `backgroundColor` property for each variant
Create a `style` object that uses the `variant` prop to return the correct styles from the `variants` object
Render the `Button` component with `"primary"` variant and `"Primary"` as children
Render the `Button` component with `"secondary"` variant and `"Secondary"` as children

name: React Query
description: Learn data fetching in React with queries, mutations, caching strategies, optimistic updates, and building performant data-driven applications
longDescription:
# Master Modern Data Fetching with React Query

**Transform your React applications from basic data fetching to professional-grade data management** with our comprehensive course that takes you from traditional useEffect patterns to mastering React Query's powerful features.

## What You'll Achieve

By the end of this course, you'll have mastered React Query (TanStack Query), the industry standard for data fetching in React applications. You'll understand how to build applications that are more performant, have better user experience, and are easier to maintain. You'll move beyond manual loading states and error handling to a declarative approach that handles caching, synchronization, and background updates automatically.

## Your Learning Journey

### **Foundation & Problem Understanding**
Start by understanding the challenges of data fetching in modern React applications. You'll see firsthand why traditional useEffect patterns become unwieldy as applications grow, and discover how React Query solves these fundamental problems. You'll learn about the mental model shift from imperative data fetching to declarative data synchronization.

### **From Traditional to Modern**
Experience the evolution of data fetching patterns by building the same functionality using useEffect, custom hooks, and finally React Query. This progression will give you deep appreciation for why React Query has become essential for modern React development and help you understand when and why to use each approach.

### **React Query Fundamentals**
Master the core concepts of React Query including the QueryClient, query keys, and the useQuery hook. You'll learn how React Query's intelligent caching system works, how it determines when data is stale, and how it manages background refetching to keep your UI synchronized with your data sources.

### **Advanced Query Patterns**
Dive deeper into React Query's powerful features including query invalidation, optimistic updates, infinite queries, and mutation handling. You'll learn to build complex data flows that feel effortless to users while being robust and predictable for developers.

### **Performance & Best Practices**
Learn the patterns and practices that separate good React Query usage from great React Query usage. Master query optimization, understand when to use different caching strategies, and learn how to structure your queries for maximum performance and maintainability.

## Who This Course Is For

- **React Developers** who want to level up their data fetching game
- **Frontend Engineers** building data-intensive applications
- **Full-stack Developers** who need better client-side data management
- **Teams** looking to standardize their data fetching patterns
- **Anyone** frustrated with manual loading states and cache management

## Your Commitment, Your Success

This course emphasizes hands-on learning with practical examples you'll use in real applications. You'll build increasingly sophisticated data fetching patterns, each building on the last, until you've mastered the complete React Query ecosystem.

**Ready to revolutionize your data fetching?** Join developers worldwide who have transformed their React applications with React Query's powerful and elegant approach to data synchronization.

modules.name: Introduction and Setup
modules.description: Learn what React Query is, understand traditional data fetching problems, and set up React Query in your application

lessons.name: What is React Query?
lessons.description: Learn what React Query is, understand the problems it solves, and explore its benefits for modern React applications
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll discover what React Query is and understand the fundamental problems it solves in modern React applications. You'll learn how React Query transforms data fetching from imperative, manual state management to a declarative, automatic approach that handles caching, synchronization, and background updates.

Your coding tasks will be to explore the code editor and get familiar with the React environment, as there are no specific code requirements for this introductory lesson.

objectives.name: Understand what React Query (TanStack Query) is and its role in modern React applications
Learn about the common problems with traditional data fetching in React (e.g., manual loading states, error handling, cache management, duplicate requests, race conditions)
Explore the benefits React Query provides (e.g., automatic caching, background refetching, optimistic updates, request deduplication, simple hooks)
Understand the mental model shift from imperative data fetching to declarative data synchronization

tasks.name: Submit the code editor – no code required

lessons.name: Fetching Data with useEffect
lessons.description: Learn the traditional approach to data fetching using useEffect and understand its limitations
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to fetch data using the traditional `useEffect` approach that most React developers are familiar with. You'll implement a basic data fetching pattern and then enhance it with loading and error states to see the manual work required.

Your coding tasks will be implementing a `useEffect` hook to fetch user data, adding loading state management, and implementing error handling to understand the complexity involved in traditional data fetching.

objectives.name: Understand how to fetch data using `useEffect` and `fetch` API
Learn to manage loading states manually during data fetching
Implement error handling for failed network requests
Recognize the boilerplate code required for basic data fetching patterns, as well as missing functionality (race conditions, cache management, etc.)

tasks.name: Replace the `user` with a state variable using `useState`
Replace the `loading` with a state variable using `useState`
Replace the `error` with a state variable using `useState`
Add a `useEffect` hook that fetches data from `API_URL` and stores it in the `user` state
Set `loading` to `true` at the start of the fetch and `false` when it completes
Add error handling by setting the `error` state to `true` if the fetch fails

lessons.name: Creating a custom useFetch hook
lessons.description: Learn to extract data fetching logic into a reusable custom hook and understand its benefits and limitations
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to create a reusable custom hook called `useFetch` that encapsulates the data fetching logic from the previous lesson. You'll see how custom hooks can reduce code duplication but still require manual state management for each use case.

Your coding tasks will be creating a `useFetch` hook that returns data, loading, and error states, then using this hook in two different components to see both its benefits and limitations.

objectives.name: Understand the naive reason why developers might want to extract data fetching logic into a custom hook without React Query

Recognize the small benefits of the custom fetch hook (reusable logic), as well as the limitations of custom fetch hooks (e.g., no caching, duplicate requests, manual cache invalidation) which haven't been solved by the custom fetch hook
Understand that this is a naive step towards fixing these problems, but that React Query does a better job by exposing the use query hook that does all of this already for you

tasks.name: Create a `useFetch` hook that takes a `url` parameter and returns an object with `data`, `loading`, and `error` properties
Use the `useFetch` hook in the `UserProfile` component to fetch user data
Remove the manual `useEffect` data fetching logic from the `UserProfile` component
Use the `useFetch` hook in the `PostsList` component to fetch posts data
Remove the manual `useEffect` data fetching logic from the `PostsList` component

lessons.name: Setting up React Query
lessons.description: Learn how to install and configure React Query with QueryClient and QueryClientProvider in your React application
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to set up React Query in your React application. You'll understand the role of the `QueryClient` and `QueryClientProvider`, and how they work together to provide React Query's functionality throughout your component tree.

Your coding tasks will be creating a `QueryClient` instance, wrapping your application with the `QueryClientProvider`, and ensuring React Query is properly configured for your app.

objectives.name: Understand the role of `QueryClient` in managing queries and mutations
Learn how `QueryClientProvider` makes React Query available throughout your component tree (reference context API)
See the basic setup pattern required for React Query integration
Understand the provider pattern and how it enables React Query's functionality

tasks.name: Import `QueryClient` and `QueryClientProvider` from `@tanstack/react-query`
Create a new `QueryClient` instance and assign it to the `queryClient` variable
Wrap the `AppContent` component with `QueryClientProvider` and pass the `queryClient` as a prop

lessons.name: Understanding the QueryClient
lessons.description: Learn about QueryClient configuration options and how to set default behavior for all queries in your application
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll dive deeper into the `QueryClient` and learn how to configure default options that will apply to all queries in your application. You'll understand key configuration options like `staleTime`, `gcTime`, and `retry` that control React Query's behavior.

Your coding tasks will be configuring the `QueryClient` with default options to customize how React Query handles caching, stale data, and retry logic across your entire application.

objectives.name: Understand the benefits of customizing the `QueryClient` in React Query at the application level
Learn about key configuration options like `staleTime` and `gcTime` (mention that these will be revisited specifically later in the course)
See some examples of use cases for different configuration options

tasks.name: Set the default `staleTime` to `5` minutes (`5 * 60 * 1000` milliseconds) in the QueryClient configuration
Set the default `gcTime` to `10` minutes (`10 * 60 * 1000` milliseconds) in the QueryClient configuration

lessons.name: Writing Your First Query
lessons.description: Learn to use the useQuery hook to fetch data and experience React Query's automatic loading and error states
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll write your first React Query using the `useQuery` hook with the simplest possible configuration. You'll see how React Query automatically handles loading states, error states, and data management without any manual state management code.

Your coding tasks will be implementing a `useQuery` hook to fetch user data and experiencing the automatic state management that React Query provides out of the box.

objectives.name: Learn the basic syntax and usage of the `useQuery` hook (without going into any options, or configuration, not even talking about query keys as they'll be covered in the next lessons)
Understand how React Query automatically provides `data`, `isLoading`, and `error` states and compare to the custom useFetch hook we did in the previous lessons
See how React Query eliminates manual state management for data fetching without having to do any extra work

Experience the simplicity of React Query compared to traditional useEffect patterns, as well as all the other benefits that React Query provides (caching, background refetching, optimistic updates, request deduplication, etc. – all for free)

tasks.name: Import `useQuery` from `@tanstack/react-query`
Use `useQuery` with the query key `["user"]` and the `fetchUser` function as the query function
Replace the `data`, `isLoading`, and `error` variables with the variables from the `useQuery` result

modules.name: Queries and Data Fetching
modules.description: Master React Query's core concepts including query keys, functions, lifecycle, states, and advanced patterns

lessons.name: Query Keys
lessons.description: Learn the anatomy of query keys, why they must be arrays, and how to structure them with and without arguments
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about query keys, which are React Query's way of uniquely identifying and caching your queries. You'll understand why query keys must be arrays and how to structure them properly, including the important rule that any arguments passed to your query function must also be included in the query key.

Your coding tasks will be creating query keys for different scenarios - one simple query without arguments and another with arguments to see how query keys change based on the data being fetched.

objectives.name: Understand that query keys must always be arrays and serve as unique identifiers for queries
Learn the anatomy of query keys and how they relate to the data being fetched
Master the rule that any arguments passed to query functions must be included in the query key
See practical examples of query keys with and without arguments (simple id arguments, but also object arguments like page, limit, etc.)

tasks.name: Create a query key `["users"]` for the `AllUsers` component
Create a query key `["user", userId]` for the `UserProfile` component using the `userId` variable

lessons.name: Query Functions
lessons.description: Learn the structure and requirements of query functions, how to pass arguments, and understand they don't have to fetch data
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about query functions - the functions that React Query calls to get your data. You'll understand that query functions must be asynchronous and can return any kind of data, not just fetched data from APIs. You'll also learn how to properly pass arguments to query functions.

Your coding tasks will be creating simple async query functions that return mock data and understanding how arguments flow from your components through to your query functions.

objectives.name: Understand that query functions must be asynchronous functions that return something

Learn that query functions don't have to fetch data - they can return any async data. Also mention that this is state management for the server state, which is what React Query is (but also understand that data fetching is the most common use case for React Query)
See how to pass arguments to query functions and ensure they're included in query keys

tasks.name: Create an async function `getProductList` that returns the `mockProducts` array after a `1` second delay
Create an async function `getProductById` that takes an `id` parameter and returns the matching product from `mockProducts`
Use the `getProductList` function in the query
Use the `getProductById` function in the query with the `productId` argument

lessons.name: The Query Lifecycle
lessons.description: Understand how queries progress through their lifecycle and the key properties available at each stage
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the query lifecycle - how a query progresses from initial mount to having data available. You'll understand that initially `data` is undefined, then `isLoading` becomes true while fetching, and finally the query resolves with either data or an error state.

Your coding tasks will be exploring the main query properties `data`, `isLoading`, and `isError` to understand how they change throughout the query lifecycle.

objectives.name: Understand that `data` starts as `undefined` when a query first mounts (reference that it's just like in useEffect, before the effect runs, the value is undefined)
Learn how `isLoading` indicates when a query is actively fetching for the first time
Learn how `isFetching` indicates when a query is actively fetching (including background fetches)
Learn how `isFetched` indicates when a query has fetched data
See how `isError` and `error` properties work when queries fail

tasks.name: Assign the `data` property from the `useQuery` hook to the `queryData` variable
Assign the `isLoading` property from the `useQuery` hook to the `isLoadingState` variable
Assign the `isFetching` property from the `useQuery` hook to the `isFetchingState` variable
Assign the `isFetched` property from the `useQuery` hook to the `isFetchedState` variable
Assign the `isError` property from the `useQuery` hook to the `isErrorState` variable
Assign the `error` property from the `useQuery` hook to the `errorState` variable

lessons.name: Fetching on Demand
lessons.description: Learn how to control when queries execute using the enabled option for on-demand data fetching
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to control when queries execute by using the `enabled` option. Sometimes you don't want a query to run immediately when a component mounts - instead, you want to trigger it based on user interaction or some other condition.

Your coding tasks will be creating a query that only runs when a user clicks a button, demonstrating how the `enabled` option gives you full control over query execution timing.

objectives.name: Understand how the `enabled` option controls query execution
Learn to create queries that only run when certain conditions are met
See how to trigger queries on-demand based on user interactions
See use cases and examples of when it makes sense to use on demand fetching in React apps and components

tasks.name: Create a state variable `shouldFetch` initialized to `false`
Add the `enabled: shouldFetch` option to the useQuery to control when it runs
Create a button that sets `shouldFetch` to `true` when clicked

lessons.name: Dependent Queries
lessons.description: Learn how to create queries that depend on data from other queries
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to create dependent queries - queries that only run after another query has completed successfully. This is useful when you need data from one query to make another query, such as fetching a user first, then fetching their posts.

Your coding tasks will be creating two queries where the second query depends on data from the first query, using the `enabled` option to control the execution flow.

objectives.name: Understand how to create queries that depend on other queries
Expand on the previous lesson to now use the `enabled` learned in the previous lesson option with data from previous queries
See how dependent queries prevent unnecessary API calls
Talk about the drawbacks of dependent queries (can be slow, need to wait for the first query to complete, etc.) and offer alternatives like parallel fetching if possible

tasks.name: Create a query to fetch user data with query key `["user"]` and the `fetchUser` function
Create a second query to fetch user posts with query key `["posts", userId]` and the `fetchUserPosts` function
Make the posts query depend on the user query by using `enabled: !!user?.id`

lessons.name: Query Custom Hooks
lessons.description: Learn to create reusable custom hooks that wrap useQuery for better code organization and reusability
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to create custom hooks that wrap `useQuery` to make your queries more reusable and organized. Custom query hooks are perfect when you want to share the same query logic, options, and configuration across multiple components.

Your coding tasks will be creating a custom `useUserQuery` hook that encapsulates user data fetching logic and then using it in multiple components to see the benefits of this pattern.

objectives.name: Understand how to wrap `useQuery` in custom hooks for reusability
Learn the benefits of custom query hooks for code organization
See how custom hooks can encapsulate query options and logic (less repetition, less prone to bugs, etc)

tasks.name: Create a custom hook `useUserQuery` that wraps and returns `useQuery`
Use the `useUserQuery` hook in the `UserCard` component
Use the `useUserQuery` hook in the `UserDetails` component

lessons.name: Query Options
lessons.description: Learn to extract and reuse query options across multiple queries while allowing customization
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to extract query options into reusable objects that can be shared across multiple queries. This approach provides flexibility - you can use the same base options but customize specific properties like `staleTime` for different use cases.

Your coding tasks will be creating a base query options object and then using it in multiple queries, with one query customizing the options to show how this pattern provides both reusability and flexibility.

objectives.name: Understand how to extract query options into reusable objects
Learn to share common query configuration across multiple queries
See how to extend and customize base query options for specific needs
Compare the query options pattern to custom hooks

tasks.name: Create a `userQueryOptions` object with `queryKey`, `queryFn` properties
Use the `userQueryOptions` in the first `useQuery`
Use the `userQueryOptions` in the second `useQuery` but override the `staleTime` to `10 * 60 * 1000`

modules.name: Mutations and Query Invalidation
modules.description: Learn to modify data with mutations, handle mutation states, and keep your UI synchronized with query invalidation

lessons.name: The useMutation hook
lessons.description: Learn the basics of the useMutation hook for modifying data and triggering side effects
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the `useMutation` hook, which is React Query's way of handling data modifications like creating, updating, or deleting data. Unlike queries that fetch data, mutations change data on the server and then often update your local cache.

Your coding tasks will be creating a simple mutation using `useMutation` and triggering it with the `mutate` function to see the basic mutation pattern in action.

objectives.name: Understand the purpose of `useMutation` for data modifications (contrast with useQuery)
Learn the basic syntax and structure of the `useMutation` hook
See how to trigger mutations using the `mutate` function

tasks.name: Import `useMutation` from `@tanstack/react-query`
Use `useMutation` with the `createUser` function and destructure the `mutate` function
Call `mutate` with the new user data when the form is submitted

lessons.name: Mutation States
lessons.description: Learn to track mutation progress using isPending and other state properties from useMutation
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about the different state properties that `useMutation` provides to track the progress of your mutations. The most important is `isPending`, which tells you when a mutation is currently running, allowing you to show loading states and disable UI elements.

Your coding tasks will be using the `isPending` state to provide user feedback during mutations and prevent duplicate submissions while a mutation is in progress.

objectives.name: Understand the `isPending` state for tracking active mutations
Learn to provide user feedback during mutation execution
See how to prevent duplicate mutations using state flags

tasks.name: Destructure the `isPending` and `isSuccess` properties from the `useMutation` hook
Disable the submit button when `isPending` is `true`
Show loading text in the button when `isPending` is `true`
Show a `p` tag with the message `"Profile updated successfully"` when `isSuccess` is `true`

lessons.name: Mutation Callbacks
lessons.description: Learn to run code at different stages of mutations using onMutate, onSuccess, and onError callbacks
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about mutation callbacks - functions that run at different stages of your mutation lifecycle. You can run code when a mutation starts (`onMutate`), when it succeeds (`onSuccess`), or when it fails (`onError`).

Your coding tasks will be adding simple callback functions that update local state to track what happens during each stage of the mutation process.

objectives.name: Understand the purpose of mutation callbacks for side effects
Learn to use `onMutate` for actions that happen before the mutation
See how `onSuccess` runs when mutations complete successfully
See how to use `onError` to handle mutation failures

tasks.name: Add an `onMutate` callback that sets the `status` state to `"mutating"`
Add an `onSuccess` callback that sets the `status` state to `"success"`
Add an `onError` callback that sets the `status` state to `"error"`

lessons.name: Invalidating Queries
lessons.description: Learn to refresh query data after mutations using useQueryClient and query invalidation
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn how to invalidate queries after mutations to keep your UI synchronized with the latest data. When you modify data on the server, you often want to refetch related queries to ensure your UI shows the most up-to-date information.

Your coding tasks will be using the `useQueryClient` hook to get access to the query client and then invalidating specific queries by their query keys in the mutation's `onSuccess` callback.

objectives.name: Understand the purpose of query invalidation for data synchronization
Learn to use the `useQueryClient` hook to access the query client
See how to invalidate queries using `queryClient.invalidateQueries`

tasks.name: Import `useQueryClient` from `@tanstack/react-query`
Use `useQueryClient` to get the `queryClient` instance
In the `onSuccess` callback, invalidate the `["todos"]` query using `queryClient.invalidateQueries`

lessons.name: Async Mutations
lessons.description: Learn to use mutateAsync for promise-based mutations that you can await and handle with async/await syntax
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll learn about `mutateAsync`, an alternative to the regular `mutate` function that returns a promise. This allows you to use async/await syntax and handle mutations in a more procedural way when you need to perform multiple operations in sequence.

Your coding tasks will be using `mutateAsync` instead of `mutate` and handling the mutation with async/await syntax to see how this pattern works for sequential operations.

objectives.name: Understand when to use `mutateAsync` instead of `mutate`
Learn to handle mutations with async/await syntax
See how to perform sequential operations after mutations

tasks.name: Destructure `mutateAsync` from the `useMutation` hook
Make the `handleSubmit` function asynchronous
Call `mutateAsync` inside a try/catch block and handle both success and error cases

modules.name: Cache Management and Invalidation
modules.description: Master React Query's powerful caching system with fine-grained control, data access, and smart invalidation strategies

lessons.name: Understanding the Query Cache
lessons.description: Learn how React Query's cache works by seeing the same query used in multiple components with instant cache hits
lessons.context:
In this lesson, you'll understand how React Query's cache works by using the same query in two different components. You'll see that when the first component fetches data, the second component can access that same data instantly from the cache without making another network request.
